speaker: interviewee
text: Prison was a different story, how I got to prison and what happened. And I told you, but I don't think it was recorded.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Let us-- let us go step by step.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what I said, it's when I came to Germany, but that was much earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So last tape we're talking about the second half of 1941. The Germans came to Lwow. And you're telling us about the changes. What sort of changes did the Germans bring to the occupation of Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were different. They were entirely different. A lot of changes. Yes. We thought that the worst is when the Russians, the Communists, came. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And because the Germans advanced from the west and we were in the east of Poland, so a lot of Jewish people, thousands of them, fled from the other side with the Germans before they came-- I mean, the Germans advanced and came to-- actually illegally, they came over to the Soviet-occupied part of Poland. They came to-- they stayed with relatives, or quite a number of them with relatives or hotels, motels, wherever they could, thousands of them. But the Russians didn't want them. So they surrounded every house at night time and got out by force those people. They were called-- in Polish, they were called [POLISH]. Not a special meaning, but because they fled from the other side here to our side. We were so-called safe, because we always-- we lived in Lwow. The other people came illegally, though the Russians surrounded house by house at night time. We could hear at night time screams, awful screams at night. I don't know why those people screamed. And they took them by force to Russia. But they sent them far to Sibir, to Siberia, and to other places in Russia very far. The children, some died. Others survived. Not like in Germany, no one killed them, but from hunger or hard work. If they could find their way a coat, they were not dressed for that cold weather there or cold climate. And I remember my mother then, when she came over to Lwow-- she came frequently from Grodek to Lwow, when the Russians were. And she said, I feel and I would like them to send us illegally. I am afraid times will come when we will regret that we didn't go to-- to Russia or Siberia, anywhere else. So how it was. She could foresee what will come. That's not the only thing that was so bad in the Russia, in the occupied of course, and when the Russians were in our place, because they-- forcibly they took people, sent them to Russia, the ones that came over from the other side, like from Warsaw, Lodz. That was the other side not near us. But we stayed, only that we couldn't get anything we wanted. We couldn't get enough food, not clothing, as I said before.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans came, did the food situation remain the same or was it worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not straight away. They left us in peace for a while. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't feel anything. We didn't know what will come. No one suspected anything. But it didn't take long that the shops were closed or taken away. And for a while, we didn't-- there was not even mentioned a ghetto and we didn't know anything. But it was harder to get food. It was hard, because as the Russians took away, took over everything, though the Germans did whatever was left. So we didn't-- it was even worse with getting food. And everybody waited. We felt that something will come, but we didn't know what. We still could visit, go like to my sister's place or to my brother's place. And nothing happened for a while. But the air, we felt it was very tense. We felt that something is coming. And everybody was frightened. Everybody sat at home, didn't go out. Of course, no mention of going gatherings, Jewish gatherings, nowhere, like synagogues or anything else. Excuse me. [COUGHS] And soon after, it started-- started talking of a ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that must've been in about end of '41, '42 begin. The middle of winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then there were separate incidents. I remember, as I said, I lived on second floor. And there were two big apartments. I was in one. And the other one was, on the same floor, was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were only two Jewish families in that building, myself with my husband, although the husband wasn't there with me, myself and my child. And another-- there was a middle-aged couple, a lawyer-- his name was Margulis-- with his wife, his son-- I think he was a university student-- from his first marriage. His wife was second wife. They had together a little girl about-- she must've been about 10 or 11 years old. And her name I remember was Halinka, because she played with my child often. She came in. And I remember that girl, that little girl [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was very fair with blue eyes. People were afraid already to walk, Jewish people, in the streets. We still felt not safe, but we had no idea what will come. It was tense, the air. And that little girl, the mother wanted her to go down and buy something. People were afraid because there were-- from time to time, we heard that someone disappeared on the street, a Jew [INAUDIBLE] so people didn't go out. That little girl, because she-- she didn't look at all Jewish. As they say, Jews should be dark or like that. She went out to buy something, never came back, although the mother was sure, because she was very much like a Gentile child, but she never came back. Two days later, maybe they questioned her, because they didn't know where Jews lived there at the beginning exactly, and especially on the street where there were not many Jews. Two gestapo men came up, rang, of course, had to be opened straight away the door for them. The gate, actually, downstairs or the entrance door. Two came up. They have seen me and my child. They didn't do anything to me and my child. They open the door. They open their door. That man, that lawyer, Margulis was there. And his son, [BACKGROUND NOISE] because young people disappeared from the street, especially male, they hid him, that boy, into-- it was a semi-bed, covered. During the day it was like a bench. You opened it, you could sleep in it at night time. He was hidden inside. They came in. They looked around. And we-- they knew already, because they questioned that girl there that they got her. They said, 'where's you son,' to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a small man. And I could see he was shaking. They said, where's your son. He said, he's not here. Where is he? He went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: They looked around and opened and got him out, took him with them, waiting. Then they took him, not his wife but him. On the second floor, we all stood- I have seen it. I couldn't look. But I just had to open door and go into the room. But I stood there. They pushed them from the second floor, that man, that Mr. Margulis, until he fell down two-- two-- from second floor right down. He was-- if he was still alive, I don't know. How he got up, I don't know. They went in and they took him and the son. What they did with him, we don't know. They killed them or sent them to Auschwitz. No one knew about concentration camps there. At the beginning, no one heard of anything. And she was left alone. And I was left with my-- still with my child. When that happened, I was scared that they'll come again. And yes, there was a talk we could hear through someone around. There lived-- there lived a Polish woman alone in one room. And she told us-- she was our neighbor, very good person, an older woman, old woman. And she said, do something about you and your child, because I've heard they come back and take away children. As a matter of fact, it's not them. The Ukrainians, as you know, they went hand with hand with Hitler. The Ukrainians were given five days, a holiday. And those five days, they had to get-- go from house to house and on the street to get the children only, to take out the children, kill them. Wherever there was a child or two or three or four, how many, they took them away and they were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she told me, if you know where to hid-- where to hide, get your child and go out of here. They will come. They go from house to house.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Right down in the cellars actually, because we didn't have then in the houses-- although it was a big house, no security doors like here. And no one had a key to the house, only it was a caretaker who lived in the-- not in the cellar, [NON-ENGLISH]. And she had a room there. When everyone rang, even midnight, she went out with a key and opened the door. For that we paid her, her or him. In our building, it was a woman, a youngish woman. She was older than I was, alone, childless. And she had one room there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she rather liked me. Oh, she was maybe a good person. And I asked her, could you take me in? There was a talk of ghetto. But people still stayed in their places. I think they took to the ghetto by streets. Our street was still there. I mean, the people were still in that street. And I asked her, could you help me? Could you take me in? Said, look, I have such a small place. But I didn't have money to pay her and she didn't ask for payment.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she took me in. She said, for a short time, I will take you, as long as they don't come here to search my place. With my child, she took me in. She took me in. We didn't talk much. It was late afternoon. I went to bed with my child into her bed. I think I told you this story before. And I don't remember if she had another bed. But on the other side, somewhere there she made herself bedding and I stayed with my child. At night time, late at night time, but he was not gestapo-- a German soldier, I don't know what rank he had-- came to her. She had friends coming into her place. Maybe that's how she made a living during, because we didn't pay her anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that German said, who is there in your bed? We haven't got a bed. Who's there? Oh, she said, my-- don't remember if she said my cousin or my-- not sister, I don't remember-- relative came from Brody or somewhere for a few days here and she stays with me with a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went up. I-- I was afraid and I covered myself. And he took up the cover, had a look at me. Maybe I didn't look to him Jewish. And he left us, went-- because more important was-- she was more important to him. So the other side, what went on, it wasn't my business. And I was still trembling inside, still thought maybe he would see a resemblance or something. And then he went. Next day, they still-- that was a time when they're looking for-- they're looking for children. But he was not Ukrainian. He was a German soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear him say anything to the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear him say anything to the woman after he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --saw you and your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But I was another day with her. Then she said, look, I was lucky, and you have to go to the ghetto. I said, but I don't want to go to the ghetto. I still-- I was myself, I didn't want to go to the ghetto. I still didn't know what will come. And my sister the other side, living the other side the street now. That was Ulica [INAUDIBLE]. She was still there with her husband and her child. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my brother not far, it was Ulica [INAUDIBLE]. I remember. He lived still there with his wife and a newly born baby, about a few weeks maybe. And so she said, I don't think that I can hold you here, hide you here longer, because [INAUDIBLE] again. He believed me. But others maybe won't.

speaker: interviewee
text: What to do? I didn't want to go to the ghetto. And my sister was still there. Then once I had a look-- yes, she said, you have to go up to your flat. I said, but could be that they'll come to me to take me from there. She said, sorry. I can't-- I wanted to help you, but I am afraid, because if-- if Gentiles were found out hiding Jews, they killed them too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went up, back to my place. And I have seen some going into the house, some Germans or Ukrainians. I don't remember who they were. I was so frightened, I don't remember even who it was. I couldn't go out, because there was raids. They go up. They just face me with a child. I go out, they come up. And I didn't know where to go, what to do. The last minute, I took the child. I don't know if she felt anything. Could be that children feel it. I Put her on the window. From the other side, one could see her through the window pane. But this time, I had very heavy, I remember dark, red, heavy curtains. I put her behind the curtain on the sill on the window, standing. I said, don't move. If she understood me, I don't know. Please don't move. You can fall out there. Stand here, here. And I covered part of the window as it is both sides hanging down, those heavy curtains. They were not white curtains on the windows, but heavy, over the curtains. And she stood behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: German came in. You have a child. Where is it? I said, they took it away from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have to think at all. Straight away I had the answers for everything. I don't know how I could think. I said, no. They took the-- and I still-- I started to cry. And I said, they took it away from me. I haven't got any. And he left. He didn't look behind the-- And as he went, I was still afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to look if she's still there or she's not on the street already, broken the window, the pane, pane of the window. She was there and I grabbed her. He didn't come back. That was once. Second time, another-- next day, again the same. They had a time when they collected only children. What to do now? I've seen two. This time, two-- I think they were officers or gestapo, whoever, because I recognized by the boots, you know. And there-- we looked over. We have seen-- we knew who they are, decorated ones. They needed decorated ones for that, for collect-- for taking away Jewish children. And again, the same thing. What to do now? If she was with them, that caretaker, they got her and she had to guide them to show where Jews lived. Even if she was a good woman, they had to do what they asked her for to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I grabbed the child. I've seen them coming in. But until they got up second floor-- on the other side of the building was-- they had entered through the entrance heavy door. She opened for them, because she had the key. On the other side, right down, the two stairs, staircases, was a door to go out on the backyard. There was not a backyard. Right down there was a door open, but it opens to the other side of the building, to the street. And the street was not a straight street you go out, but it was a bit hilly, high up, right down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so when you opened the door-- the door was open always-- you go out. It wasn't locked. I don't know why. But you can-- you could go down to the street there. As I ran with my child quickly down the two stairs-- I was young. And still, even if I was under-nourished but still strong enough, I kept the child, went-- ran down quickly second floor. And I was afraid they would still come, see me. I went out and I thought I'd go out to the other side. I didn't, because if they-- I just felt it. If I go out, they will go after me. They will find me. But as I went out-- we have it here too under the stairs, there is a space, a little space where bins for garbage, there were about two big, like here we have big bins for garbage. There was not much room. But I took my child, squeezed myself in before-- behind the garbage bins in the corner. I think she stopped breathing. She understood. Even as such a young child, she under-- she stopped breathing. And I said, hold on to me. Hold on. I went-- I can see it. It was like a little triangle open there to go in. But it was so-- not very deep. I couldn't go in and hide very-- be safe there. But I did squeeze myself in. And pushed the two garbage bins in front of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't think of anything, because I heard them coming down. And I heard asked-- those two, I think they were gestapo men, they asked her, where could she go with the-- with that little child? Where could she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I don't know. But they said, her door's open. I could-- in German, I understood. He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I could see them. I could see their boots shining, the boots from the corner. It was like this. I sit here. And here were the two bins in front of me. And I was sitting there squeezing myself back to the wall, and my child on my side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they talked to her. And she said, where could she go? The door's open, Herr, from the room, so she must be here somewhere. But she said, she's not. She must have-- that she did. She helped me. She must have run out here, so they couldn't go back. If they would go back, it was-- I don't believe in miracles, but many times it happened to me like a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: If he would-- they would go back, they would see me, face-to-face. They were here and I was here, hidden in that-- squeezing myself to the wall. So-- but she said, she must have gone out there. So they went out and never came back. And as they went out, I was-- I felt safe. I was afraid, oh, maybe they will come back. And I thought, no. I didn't move. I didn't go out. They might-- they could come back. But they didn't, because she let them out the other side. Said, she must have gone out there. See, the door's open. So she helped me. And I have seen them with the green uniforms and I have seen their boots shining, because I was sitting here and I have seen as they pass- they passed just like this near me. But they never came back. Then I waited for a while. I was afraid to go up. They wouldn't come back, because if they went from house to house-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was waiting and waiting, and I didn't go out. But two days later, I wanted to see if my sister-- we were a very close family. And her child is still alive, though I thought two days later, they finished here, they go further, because they went from section to sections, from one place to another, street to street. And my sister was living like [INAUDIBLE], outside-- the other side of the street. I had to go through one street to the other, not far. I walked. I took my child by hand. She walked with me. And went-- went out only of the house. As I went out, three tall men, Ukrainians-- they helped the Russ-- the Germans-- just singing, going down-- it was a street just a bit high up, down rather. They went down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there with my little child by hand. One came over to me. He said, where did you hide with your child? He wanted to take her away.

speaker: interviewee
text: She clung to me to the other side and hold me tight. She was afraid. She understood. I don't know how. I think children got much older then than they were really. And he wanted to tear her away from me. I couldn't speak. I couldn't utter a word. He was with two more Ukraine-- one of them said-- I was lucky-- leave her alone. It's over. The five-- our five days are over. That was after the five days. Leave her alone. Let her child stay with her mother, one only. They left me, went down further. It was just-- although the time was over, they got only five days to do it, but it was fun for them. And I went over to my sister's place. I went over to my sister's place. They were still there with the child. Their caretaker, a woman too, helped them too, the same thing, a Polish woman. Like mine helped me, the other one helped them. They were still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the caretaker, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of their caretaker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, if I would remember-- I was in Poland back. I'll tell you later about this. That was after. I was a few times back to-- not to Lwow, because I couldn't go to Lwow. It was still then Russia. I couldn't go with a false name. I was still a Grabowienska. I couldn't go there back. But I've heard later-- my sister told me in the ghetto later-- we went to the ghetto later. And I was only two weeks in the ghetto. She said, I knew once her name, but I don't remember now. She took away from everything. She took her flat. She took her furniture. She took a beautiful home she had. Everything she took from her. But my sister said, if you help me, I'll give you anything you want to. Take out anything you want. But she took away everything later. She helped her, but she took away everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of your caretaker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think I knew her name ever. No one was interested to know the name of a caretaker then. She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time-- what time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I think the ghetto was established in Lwow in end of '41, something like that, end of '41 when the Russians left. And they took-- that was only a short time, a week after, a week and how it happened. And we had to go to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed as long as I could there, so did my sister, because the caretaker helped us. But I don't-- we were never interested in the name of the caretaker. So it was already-- winter was starting.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Winter was already starting at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then it started, that-- the ghetto was established a little bit earlier. Some people from other parts of Lwow nearer to the ghetto got there. But from the distant parts of Lwow, we went later to the ghetto. And when it got very bad, we had to go, so we tried not to. So we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue the story next time.
